text:
Almost two decades into democracy, churches of Reformed affiliation in South Africa appear to be beset by withdrawal and an uncertainty regarding their role in the public space. The apparent prophetic silence of these churches coincides with a time when corruption as a major threat to development and equity is a pervasive challenge. This article reflects on the churches’ current crisis of engagement in terms of their respective theological epistemologies and their attendant understandings of the church’s space, with a view to facilitating a fresh engagement with the public space.

In this article, the author examines three of the primary frameworks that have been used to describe how the church might address corruption, namely the Two Kingdoms Model, the Neo-Calvinist Approach and the Communicative Rationality Approach. By applying each of the frameworks to the example of corruption, he investigates their understandings of the role of the church in public life. His evaluation suggests that dualism threatens to leave the church invisible to the world and organic (integral) approaches collapse the distinctiveness of the Christian faith, with neither sufficient to enable the church to carry out a genuine prophetic ministry except insofar as each can be located within a pneumatological union with Christ. The author then describes what is required to carry out a truly public theology and makes a call for churches to move beyond defensiveness and assertiveness to an identity that can form its life around a renewed vision of transforming society through justice and ethics, leading to life in all its fullness.
